message.content:
## Synthesis: research report

**Question:** Best algorithms/techniques to make audio the most comprehensible (e.g. time-scale modification like WSOLA/PSOLA, intelligibility enhancement, silence/pause compression, spectral and prosody processing), AND how "speed listening" works at playback speeds greater than 16x — the perceptual limits of accelerated speech comprehension, training/adaptation effects, and the signal-processing methods that make extreme speed-ups intelligible.

20 claims survived 3-vote adversarial verification. Merge semantic duplicates and synthesize.

## Confirmed claims
### [0] Time-scale modification (TSM) is the task of changing an audio signal's playback speed (speeding up or slowing down) without changing its pitch, which is the foundational operation behind speed-listening playback.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.audiolabs-erlangen.de/content/05_fau/professor/00_mueller/06_projects/90_siamus/2016_DriedgerMueller_TSMOverview_AppliedSciences_ePrint.pdf (primary)
Quote: "Time-scale modification (TSM) is the task of speeding up or slowing down an audio signal's playback speed without changing its pitch."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim accurately restates the canonical, verbatim definition from the cited primary source (Driedger & Müller, "A Review of Time-Scale Modification of Music Signals," MDPI Applied Sciences 6(2):57, 2016): "Time-scale modification (TSM) is the task of speeding up or slowing down an audio signal's playback speed without changing its pitch." This is a peer-reviewed review by recognized audio-signal-processing authorities at International Audio Laboratories Erlangen — source strength matches the (modest) claim. Independently corroborated by Stanford CCRMA (J.O. Smith, Spectral Audio Signal Processing): TSM "means speeding up or slowing down a sound without affecting the frequency content, such as the perceived pitch." No credible source disputes this; it is textbook-standard terminology and not outdated (definition is stable). The only part going beyond the literal quote is "foundational operation behind speed-listening," but this is a sound, well-supported inference — multiple sources confirm pitch-preserving playback speedup (podcasts/audiobooks at 1.5x-2x) is exactly TSM, and that WSOLA/PSOLA are the classic algorithms used. Not a marketing/forum/cherry-picked claim. The cited Erlangen PDF is the author-hosted ePrint of this same MDPI paper.

### [1] Classical TSM methods WSOLA and the phase vocoder (PV-TSM) preserve perceptual quality of harmonic signals well but introduce noticeable artifacts on percussive signals; no single method handles all signal types, so combining approaches reduces artifacts.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.audiolabs-erlangen.de/content/05_fau/professor/00_mueller/06_projects/90_siamus/2016_DriedgerMueller_TSMOverview_AppliedSciences_ePrint.pdf (primary)
Quote: "classical TSM procedures based on waveform similarity overlap-add (WSOLA) [4] or on the phase vocoder (PV-TSM) [5–7] are capable of preserving the perceptual quality of harmonic signals to a high degree, but introduce noticeable artifacts when modifying percussive signals. However, it is possible to substantially reduce artifacts by combining different TSM approaches."
Verifier evidence (high): Verified against the primary source by extracting the full PDF text (Driedger & Müller, "A Review of Time-Scale Modification of Music Signals," Appl. Sci. 2016, 6, 57; MDPI Applied Sciences; International Audio Laboratories Erlangen). The supporting quote is VERBATIM in the paper's introduction: "waveform similarity overlap-add (WSOLA) [4] or on the phase vocoder (PV-TSM) [5-7] are capable of preserving the perceptual quality of harmonic signals to a high degree, but introduce noticeable artifacts when modifying percussive signals. However, it is possible to substantially reduce artifacts by combining different TSM approaches." The claim's added gloss "no single method handles all signal types" is also directly stated in the abstract: "there is no single TSM method that can cope with all kinds of audio signals equally well." The paper's Section 6 details the HPS-based fusion (Driedger 2014, IEEE Sig. Proc. Lett.) that applies different TSM to harmonic vs percussive components. Independent web search corroborates the underlying mechanisms (PV-TSM causes transient smearing/phasiness; WSOLA causes transient doubling on polyphonic/percussive material). I tried to refute on all five checklist axes and could not: (1) quote is exact, no overreach; (2) no contradicting source — this is consensus DSP knowledge; (3) peer-reviewed review by the field's authoritative authors matches the modest claim strength; (4) 2016 but TSM fundamentals are mature/stable, claim is descriptive of unchanging signal-processing physics; (5) not marketing/benchmark/forum.

### [2] WSOLA improves comprehensibility over plain OLA by allowing small shifts (up to plus or minus delta_max samples) of analysis-frame positions, choosing the shift that maximizes cross-correlation so periodic (harmonic) structures align in overlapping regions and phase-jump artifacts are reduced.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.audiolabs-erlangen.de/content/05_fau/professor/00_mueller/06_projects/90_siamus/2016_DriedgerMueller_TSMOverview_AppliedSciences_ePrint.pdf (primary)
Quote: "This method's core idea is to allow for slight shifts (of up to ±∆max ∈ Z samples) of the analysis frames' positions... The main idea is to adjust successive synthesis frames in such a way that periodic structures in the frames' waveforms are aligned in the overlapping regions."
Verifier evidence (high): Every clause of the claim is verbatim/near-verbatim supported by the primary source (Driedger & Müller, "TSM of Audio Signals: A Review," Applied Sciences 2016, 6:57, Section 4). Extracted from the actual PDF text: (1) WSOLA vs OLA improvement — "One of OLA's problems is that it lacks signal sensitivity... One time-domain strategy to reduce phase jump artifacts as caused by OLA is to introduce some flexibility in the TSM process, achieved by allowing some tolerance in the analysis or synthesis frames' positions." OLA "is in general not capable of preserving local periodic structures... These distortions are also known as phase jump artifacts." (2) ±delta_max shifts — exact: "This method's core idea is to allow for slight shifts (of up to ±∆max ∈ Z samples) of the analysis frames' positions" with "∆m ∈ [−∆max : ∆max]." (3) Maximize cross-correlation — exact: "We can then compute the optimal shift index ∆m+1 that maximizes the cross-correlation of x̃m and xm" (defined via argmax of the cross-correlation metric). (4) Align periodic structures in overlap region — exact: "find the optimal shift index ∆m+1 ... such that periodic structures of the adjusted analysis frame x'm+1 are optimally aligned with structures of the previously copied synthesis frame ym in the overlapping region." (5) Reduce phase-jump artifacts — stated explicitly as the goal (above). No contradicting evidence found; the original WSOLA paper (Verhelst & Roelands, IEEE 1993) and multiple independent IEEE/ISCA sources corroborate the waveform-similarity/cross-correlation maximization mechanism. Source is a peer-reviewed tutorial by established DSP researchers — quality exceeds the (non-extraordinary, foundational) claim's strength. Not outdated (WSOLA is a stable 1993 algorithm), not marketing/forum. The claim is a faithful, accurate paraphrase, if anything slightly under-stating the source.

### [3] Trained blind/vision-impaired individuals can learn to comprehend accelerated (time-compressed) speech at rates up to ~22 syllables per second, roughly 3x the normal speaking rate of ~6 syl/s.
Vote: 2-1 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/ (primary)
Quote: "vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)"
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is well-supported by the cited primary source and a corroborating source. PMC3805979 (Dietrich, Hertrich, Ackermann, "Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans," Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 2013) states verbatim: "vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)." A second peer-reviewed paper by the same group (PMC3847124, BMC Neuroscience fMRI study) independently states "blind people [can] understand spoken language at speaking rates of up to 22 syllables (syl) per second – an accomplishment exceeding by far the upper limits of untrained subjects (ca. 8 syl/s)." That same paper gives normal speaking rate as "4-6 syl/s," so the claim's "~6 syl/s normal" is within range (upper edge). The "up to ~22" wording is faithful to the literature, which presents 22 syl/s as the upper limit reached by highly proficient trained individuals (originally from Moos & Trouvain 2007). Minor imprecision: 22/6 ≈ 3.7x, so "roughly 3x" slightly understates the ratio (though it matches 22/8 ≈ 2.75x untrained ceiling); the "roughly" hedge keeps this within acceptable bounds and does not constitute a misrepresentation. No contradicting evidence found in searches. Source quality (peer-reviewed neuroscience, two independent papers) matches claim strength; not marketing/forum/cherry-picked. Field is stable (established psychoacoustics), so 2013 date is not problematic.

### [4] Months of daily training (via a text-to-speech screen reader) produces measurable adaptation: four of six trained blind participants reached ~70% word comprehension at an ultra-fast 18 syl/s rate.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/ (primary)
Quote: "considerable enhancement of ultra-fast speech perception (about 70% points correctly repeated words)"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is directly and faithfully supported by the primary peer-reviewed source: Dietrich, Hertrich & Ackermann, "Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans," Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 2013 (PMC3805979). The abstract states verbatim: "Four of six participants showed—independently from residual visual functions—considerable enhancement of ultra-fast speech perception (about 70% points correctly repeated words)." Verified specifics: training lasted ~6 months at >=1 hour daily using the JAWS screen reader with the Eloquence TTS synthesizer (supports "months of daily training via a text-to-speech screen reader"); 18 syl/s is explicitly labeled "ultra-fast"; pre-training comprehension at 18 syl/s was below ~14% (so the ~70% post-training figure is a genuine, large adaptation effect, not baseline). Comprehension was measured via a sentence-repetition task scored as percentage of syllables in correctly reproduced words; the claim's wording "70% word comprehension" mirrors the abstract's own phrase "70% points correctly repeated words," so it is not an overreach. The figure was corroborated across multiple independent mirrors (PMC, frontiersin.org, PubMed PMID 24167485). One minor imprecision: the cohort was 5 blind + 1 normal-sighted subject, so "six trained blind participants" is slightly inaccurate, but this does not affect the verified "four of six reached ~70%" result and is immaterial to the claim's substance.

### [5] Time-compressed speech (compressed by the sampling method) yields significantly higher speech-rate intelligibility thresholds than simple speeded speech, meaning sampling-based compression preserves intelligibility at faster rates than naive speed-up.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/bf03199702 (primary)
Quote: "The first experiment compared thresholds of two types of rapid speech reportedly different in intelligibility: simple speeded speech and speech compressed by the sampling method. ... Thresholds for compressed speech were significantly higher than those for speeded speech, an indication that the threshold at the very least reflects intelligibility."
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED, not refuted. The source is H.J. de Haan (1977), "A speech-rate intelligibility threshold for speeded and time-compressed connected speech," Perception & Psychophysics 22:366-372 (Springer, peer-reviewed primary source) — confirmed via the Springer DOI and DTIC citation ADA061172. (1) Quote support: the article states "Thresholds for compressed speech were significantly higher than those for speeded speech, an indication that the threshold at the very least reflects intelligibility" — directly matching the claim. (2) Direction is correct: the threshold is defined as "the maximum rate of speech understood by individual listeners," so a HIGHER threshold means speech remains intelligible at a FASTER rate. Thus "higher threshold for compressed speech" correctly means sampling-based compression preserves intelligibility at faster rates than naive speeded speech — the claim's interpretation is faithful, not an overreach. (3) Mechanism matches: the "sampling method" (periodic removal of speech segments, pitch-preserving) vs "simple speeded speech" (pitch-raising playback speed-up) corresponds exactly to the claim's "sampling-based compression vs naive speed-up." (4) Independently corroborated by multiple retrievals (search summaries and a Google Scholar snippet quoting "found a higher threshold for compressed than for speeded speech"). (5) No contradicting source surfaced; the finding aligns with the still-current consensus that pitch-preserving time-scale modification outperforms naive resampling for intelligibility. Minor caveats: the study is from 1977 (foundational, not a fast-moving-tech claim) and its primary aim was validating the threshold metric rather than ranking methods, but the directional finding is accurately represented.

### [6] Words that are individually intelligible become less intelligible when presented inside the compressed time constraints of a fast message, indicating a perceptual/processing-time bottleneck under compression.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/BF03208200 (primary)
Quote: "individually compressed words are less intelligible when presented within the limited time constraints of a compressed message"
Verifier evidence (high): Source confirmed as Heiman, Leo, Leighbody & Bowler (1986), "Word intelligibility decrements and the comprehension of time-compressed speech," Perception & Psychophysics 40(6):407-411, DOI 10.3758/BF03208200 (PubMed PMID 3808907; Springer link in claim). The article title alone directly supports the claim. Multiple independent renderings of the article content confirm the supporting quote: the study (Experiment 2, backward-masking procedure) "demonstrated that individually compressed words are less intelligible when presented within the limited time constraints of a compressed message," concluding that decreased comprehension of compressed messages results from decreased word intelligibility. This is a faithful match to the claim — not an overreach — including the framing as a processing-time/perceptual bottleneck under compression (limited time constraints reduce intelligibility of words that are individually intelligible). Checklist: (1) Quote supports claim and matches article title. (2) WebSearch found NO contradicting source; related literature on gated/concatenated and interrupted speech is consistent, not contradictory. (3) Source is a peer-reviewed primary research article on exactly this question — quality matches claim strength. (4) Age (1986) is the only weakness, but this is a foundational human-perception/psychophysics finding, not a fast-moving tech field, and remains uncontested. (5) Not marketing/forum/press-release. Could not refute.

### [7] Normal-hearing listeners can perceive time-compressed speech up to roughly 11 to 16 syllables per second, defining an approximate perceptual ceiling for accelerated speech comprehension.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9085996/ (primary)
Quote: "The ability to perceive time-compressed speech in normal-hearing listeners has been reported to be approximately 11 to 16 syllables per second."
Verifier evidence (high): Quote verbatim in primary source (Gransier et al. 2022, PMC9085996): "The ability to perceive time-compressed speech in normal-hearing listeners has been reported to be approximately 11 to 16 syllables per second (Versfeld and Dreschler 2002; Meng et al. 2019)." Independently corroborated by Nature Scientific Reports (s41598-023-29755-x): "the upper limit of young normal-hearing listeners to perceive 50% of time-compressed speech ranges from 11 to 16 syllables per second." Claim correctly scoped to normal-hearing listeners and hedged ("roughly," "approximate"). Blind-listener literature (PMC3805979) gives ~8 syl/sec untrained, ~22 syl/sec trained — consistent, not contradictory. Multiple peer-reviewed sources, current, not marketing. Minor caveat: source says "reported ability range" vs claim's "ceiling," but Nature corroborator uses "upper limit."</parameter>
<parameter name="counterSource">https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-29755-x; https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/</parameter>
</invoke>


### [8] The study time-compressed speech using the Pitch Synchronous Overlap and Add (PSOLA) algorithm, chosen because it shifts the modulation spectrum toward higher modulation frequencies as the compression ratio increases.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9085996/ (primary)
Quote: "The LIST sentences were uniformly time-compressed at seven different compression ratios (CRs) with the Pitch Synchronous Overlap and Add algorithm (PSOLA)...The PSOLA algorithm was used as it shifts the modulation spectrum with increasing compression ratio to higher modulation frequencies."
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is faithfully supported by the primary source (Gransier, van Wieringen & Wouters, 2022, "The Intelligibility of Time-Compressed Speech Is Correlated with the Ability to Listen in Modulated Noise," J. Assoc. Res. Otolaryngol., PMC9085996). The article states verbatim: "The LIST sentences were uniformly time-compressed at seven different compression ratios (CRs) with the Pitch Synchronous Overlap and Add algorithm (PSOLA)" and "The PSOLA algorithm was used as it shifts the modulation spectrum with increasing compression ratio to higher modulation frequencies, in contrast to non-uniform time-compression algorithms, but at the same time preserves the shape of modulation spectrum and the spectral features of the speech signal." The claim accurately paraphrases this rationale (it does omit the "preserves spectral features / contrast to non-uniform algorithms" qualifier, but this is not an overreach). The underlying signal-processing physics is independently corroborated: uniform time compression increases modulation frequencies, and PSOLA is a standard pitch-synchronous method preserving pitch contour and spectral features (Wikipedia, Audio time stretching and pitch scaling). Source is a 2022 peer-reviewed primary research article in a reputable journal indexed on PMC — strength matches claim. No contradicting evidence found.

### [9] Special populations can perceive speech at rates up to 22 syllables per second, indicating that the neural processing of time-compressed speech can be altered by exposure and training (adaptation effects).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9085996/ (primary)
Quote: "It is not uncommon that special populations perceive speech at rates up to 22 syllables per second...indicating that the neural processing mechanisms of time-compressed speech can be altered by, for example, exposure and training."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is well-supported and accurately quotes the primary source. The exact sentence appears in Gransier, van Wieringen & Wouters (2022), "The Intelligibility of Time-Compressed Speech Is Correlated with the Ability to Listen in Modulated Noise," J. Assoc. Res. Otolaryngology (PMC9085996): "It is not uncommon that special populations perceive speech at rates up to 22 syllables per second...indicating that the neural processing mechanisms of time-compressed speech can be altered by, for example, exposure and training." The 22 syl/s figure is independently corroborated by a separate body of peer-reviewed neuroscience (Dietrich/Hertrich/Ackermann group): the fMRI study "Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects..." (PMC3847124) states that "repeated exposure to accelerated verbal utterances may enable blind people to understand spoken language at speaking rates of up to 22 syllables per second," versus ~8 syl/s for sighted listeners, and shows right primary visual cortex/fusiform gyrus activation "covaried with the capability of ultra-fast spoken language comprehension" — direct evidence of training/exposure-driven neural plasticity. A 2013 Frontiers training study ("Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization...") further confirms adaptation effects. No contradicting source found. Minor caveat (does not refute): 22 syl/s is the learned upper bound for blind populations; many individual experiments tested at 16 syl/s, but the claim correctly scopes the figure to special populations and to exposure/training, matching the literature.

### [10] Time compression of speech in this study was performed using the WSOLA (Waveform Similarity Overlap and Add) algorithm, which preserves pitch while shortening duration and achieves high-quality time-scale modification of speech.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3467231/ (primary)
Quote: "Time compression throughout the experiment was carried out using an implementation of the WSOLA (Waveform Similarity Overlap and Add) algorithm, which has been shown to achieve very high quality time-scale modification of speech signals."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is verbatim-supported by the source. Direct fetch of PMC3467231 ("Perceptual Learning of Time-Compressed Speech: More than Rapid Adaptation") confirms the exact text: "Time compression throughout the experiment was carried out using an implementation of the WSOLA (Waveform Similarity Overlap and Add) algorithm, which has been shown to achieve very high quality time-scale modification of speech signals." The article body further confirms WSOLA "maintains acoustic properties like pitch and timbre unchanged," directly supporting the claim's pitch-preservation assertion. Independent authoritative sources corroborate WSOLA's properties: the original Verhelst & Roelands (1993) IEEE ICASSP / Eurospeech papers ("An overlap-add technique based on waveform similarity (WSOLA) for high quality time-scale modification of speech") establish it as a pitch-preserving, high-quality TSM method that speeds up/slows down audio without altering pitch. No credible source disputes or qualifies this. Source is a peer-reviewed PMC-indexed article; the claim is a modest, factual methods description (not an extraordinary claim), so source quality matches claim strength. WSOLA is a stable, still-standard algorithm, so the claim is not outdated, and this is academic methods text, not marketing. Tried and failed to refute on all five checklist axes.

### [11] Comprehension of time-compressed speech improves rapidly with brief exposure (10-20 sentences), but this rapid adaptation leaves performance well below ceiling, indicating brief adaptation alone is insufficient for full intelligibility at high compression.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3467231/ (primary)
Quote: "Brief adaptation (10–20 sentences) significantly improved identification, but left performance well below perfect (40–85% correct)."
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED — could not refute. The supporting quote is verbatim from the primary source (PMC3467231 = PLOS One 10.1371/journal.pone.0047099, Banai & Lavner / "Perceptual Learning of Time-Compressed Speech: More than Rapid Adaptation"). The article states: "Brief adaptation (10–20 sentences) significantly improved identification, but left performance well below perfect (40–85% correct)" (initial reconstruction was only 20–76% of words at 30–45% compression). The claim's interpretation is not an overreach — it IS the paper's central thesis, reflected in its title. The authors explicitly conclude that "Multiday training on time-compressed speech results in significantly more perceptual learning than brief adaptation," with trained listeners eventually matching native performance, confirming brief adaptation alone is insufficient for full intelligibility. (1) Quote supports claim verbatim. (2) No contradicting source found — corroborated by PubMed 23056592 and the broader two-phase perceptual-learning literature (e.g., on-line plasticity studies PMC2775905, cross-linguistic adaptation work). (3) Source is peer-reviewed primary research, matching the modest claim strength. (4) ~2012 publication, but this is stable psychoacoustics/perceptual-learning science about human perceptual limits, not a fast-moving field. (5) Not marketing — academic finding with a nuanced, partly-null conclusion (adaptation is NOT enough). All five checklist items pass.

### [12] Extended multiday training produces substantially more perceptual learning of time-compressed speech than brief single-session adaptation, showing that adaptation to accelerated speech has both a rapid phase and a slower training-dependent phase.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3467231/ (primary)
Quote: "Multiday training on time-compressed speech results in significantly more perceptual learning than brief adaptation."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is a near-verbatim restatement of the primary source's central finding. The paper is Banai & Lavner (2012), PLoS ONE, "Perceptual Learning of Time-Compressed Speech: More than Rapid Adaptation" (PMC3467231 / PubMed 23056592 / DOI 10.1371/journal.pone.0047099) — a peer-reviewed controlled study. Abstract states: "Multiday training on time-compressed speech results in significantly more perceptual learning than brief adaptation." The two-phase structure in the claim is directly supported: learning "progress[es] from an initial, rapid adaptation phase to a subsequent prolonged and more stimulus specific phase." Quantitative backing: trained listeners improved ~0.24 threshold units (Cohen's d = 1.37) vs untrained controls ~0.10 units (d = 0.56) — more than double the effect with extended multiday (five-session) training. Design: two groups of non-native Hebrew speakers tested 10-14 days apart, one group receiving 5 practice sessions in between; trained group reached native-speaker-equivalent performance on trained conditions. Checklist results: (1) quote fully supports claim, no overreach; (2) WebSearch found no contradicting source — finding is consistent with broader perceptual-learning literature; (3) source quality (primary peer-reviewed) matches claim strength; (4) 2012 date is fine for a stable human-perception finding, not a fast-moving field; (5) not marketing — academic study with reported effect sizes and controls. The only nuance (training gains are more stimulus-specific than the broadly-generalizing rapid phase) refines rather than refutes the claim.

### [13] The review establishes that comprehension of accelerated (time-compressed) speech begins to decline at approximately 275 words per minute, a threshold widely attributed to this 1969 paper and tied to the processing capacity of the phonological loop.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.academia.edu/115224083/Review_of_research_on_the_intelligibility_and_comprehension_of_accelerated_speech (primary)
Quote: "previous work has indicated that speech comprehension begins to decline at around 275 words per minute (Foulke & Sticht, 1969) ... when information is presented at speeds that surpass what the phonological loop ... can handle (275 words per minute; see Foulke & Sticht, 1969)"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim faithfully restates its supporting quote and is corroborated by multiple independent sources. (1) The 275 wpm threshold is robustly and widely attributed to Foulke & Sticht (1969), "Review of research on the intelligibility and comprehension of accelerated speech," Psychological Bulletin 72:50-62 — the very academia.edu document cited (ID 115224083, PubMed 4897155 confirms authorship/year). Multiple independent sources confirm: "Foulke and Sticht (1969)... rate of auding comprehension declines slowly as word rate is increased, up to a rate of some 275 wpm; beyond this point, comprehension declines more rapidly." (2) The phonological-loop framing is genuinely present in the literature: the 2024 Educational Psychology Review paper (Springer, doi 10.1007/s10648-024-09917-7) states verbatim "when information is presented at speeds that surpass what the phonological loop, which channels auditory information, can handle (275 words per minute; see Foulke & Sticht, 1969)." So the claim's two assertions — (a) threshold attributed to the 1969 paper, (b) tied to phonological-loop capacity — each map to real source text. Minor caveats that do NOT rise to refutation: (i) the 1969 finding is technically that decline ACCELERATES at 275 wpm (comprehension already declines slowly before it), so "begins to decline at 275" is a slight oversimplification — but the citing quote itself uses "begins to decline," so the claim accurately reflects its source; (ii) the phonological loop (Baddeley & Hitch) was not introduced until 1974, so the loop interpretation is a modern theoretical overlay by the 2024 authors, not Foulke & Sticht's own framing — but the claim correctly presents these as two separate facts ("attributed to this 1969 paper AND tied to... the phonological loop") rather than claiming the 1969 paper invoked the loop. Source quality (peer-reviewed Psychological Bulletin original + peer-reviewed 2024 Springer review) matches the claim's strength. Cannot refute.

### [14] The paper reviews methods for generating time-compressed speech and methods for assessing the effect of compression on word intelligibility and listening comprehension of connected discourse, distinguishing intelligibility from comprehension.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.academia.edu/115224083/Review_of_research_on_the_intelligibility_and_comprehension_of_accelerated_speech (primary)
Quote: "Methods for generating time-compressed speech are reviewed, along with methods for assessing the effect of compression on word intelligibility and listening comprehension, including experiments on word intelligibility and comprehensibility of connected discourse."
Verifier evidence (high): Claim accurately describes Foulke & Sticht (1969), "Review of research on the intelligibility and comprehension of accelerated speech," Psychological Bulletin 72(1):50-62 (PMID 4897155, DOI 10.1037/h0027575). The academia.edu source URL was 403-blocked, but the paper's abstract was verified independently via ResearchGate (publication 18271848) and corroborated by PubMed citation metadata and multiple citing works. The abstract explicitly states: (1) "Methods for the generation of time compressed speech are reviewed" — supports the generation-methods claim; (2) the paper reviews "intelligibility and comprehension of accelerated speech," covering effects on "word intelligibility" and "comprehensibility of connected discourse" — supports the assessment-methods claim; (3) "The general finding that compression in time has a different effect upon the comprehensibility of connected discourse than upon word intelligibility is discussed" — directly supports the intelligibility-vs-comprehension distinction. Multiple independent sources describe this as the seminal/foundational work establishing exactly this distinction (e.g., Springer-indexed follow-up studies on "word intelligibility decrements and the comprehension of time-compressed speech"). No source disputes or qualifies the claim. Source quality (peer-reviewed APA review journal) matches the claim's modest strength; the claim merely describes the paper's scope, which is not an extraordinary claim. Minor caveat: it is a 1969 paper predating WSOLA/PSOLA/modern DSP, but the claim does not assert otherwise.

### [15] Trained late-blind individuals can comprehend ultra-fast synthetic (screen-reader) speech at rates up to about 22 syllables per second, far exceeding sighted listeners' capacities.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/human-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnhum.2013.00701/full (primary)
Quote: "vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)—exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is directly supported by the cited primary source. The Frontiers in Human Neuroscience article (Dietrich et al., 2013, "Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans," DOI 10.3389/fnhum.2013.00701) states verbatim: "vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)—exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s." Every element of the claim checks out: (a) "trained late-blind individuals" matches the article's title and population; (b) "up to about 22 syl/s" is a direct quote; (c) "far exceeding sighted listeners" matches the stated ~8 syl/s sighted ceiling. The 22 syl/s figure traces to the original empirical source, Moos & Trouvain (2007), "Comprehension of ultra-fast speech – blind vs. 'normally hearing' persons," Proc. 16th ICPhS, Saarbrücken, pp. 677-680, and is independently echoed in a companion fMRI study (Dietrich et al., BMC Neuroscience 2013, 14:74, PMC3847124). No credible source disputing or refuting this figure surfaced in searches. The claim correctly frames 22 syl/s as a peak/upper-bound for trained users ("up to about"), not a typical rate, so it is not an overreach. Source quality (multiple peer-reviewed publications) matches the claim's strength. Minor non-fatal caveat: the original empirical work used time-compressed natural speech and formant synthesis as well as TTS, but "synthetic (screen-reader) speech" is consistent with the practical application emphasized throughout these papers.

### [16] Some blind individuals can comprehend accelerated synthetic speech at rates up to about 22 syllables per second, roughly 3x the ~8 syllables/second ceiling of sighted listeners.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3847124/ (primary)
Quote: "repeated exposure to accelerated verbal utterances may enable blind people to understand spoken language at speaking rates of up to 22 syllables (syl) per second"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is verbatim-supported by the primary source (Dietrich et al., BMC Neuroscience 2013, PMC3847124). The abstract states: "repeated exposure to accelerated verbal utterances may enable blind people to understand spoken language at speaking rates of up to 22 syllables (syl) per second" and that this exceeds "the upper limits of untrained subjects (ca. 8 syl/s)." 22/8 = 2.75x, faithfully described as "roughly 3x." The claim's hedges ("some blind individuals," "up to about 22") match the source's "may enable" and "up to," with no overreach. It is correctly framed as a trained-comprehension capability (not mere exposure). Corroborated by independent peer-reviewed work from the same group (Frontiers in Human Neuroscience 2013, PMC3805979; PLOS One 2015) and mainstream coverage (Scientific American, "Why Can Some Blind People Process Speech Far Faster Than Sighted Persons?"). No contradicting or heavily-qualifying source found. Caveat: the study's own fMRI testing used 16 syl/s; 22 syl/s is the reported ceiling for the most proficient subjects, which the claim's "up to about 22" wording correctly reflects.

### [17] On ultra-fast speech at 16 syllables/second, sighted subjects scored consistently below 16% accuracy while blind subjects averaged about 59.7% (range 0-93%), demonstrating a large blind-vs-sighted gap at extreme speeds.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3847124/ (primary)
Quote: "sighted performance on ultra-fast (16 syl/s) speech "consistently fell below 16%" accuracy, versus blind subjects ranging from 0-93% (mean 59.7%)"
Verifier evidence (high): Primary source (Dietrich, Hertrich & Ackermann, BMC Neuroscience 2013; PMC3847124, "Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar") fully supports the claim verbatim. At 16 syl/s ultra-fast speech: SIGHTED subjects' "performance level consistently fell below 16% (mean = 9.0%, SD +/-4.7%)"; BLIND subjects ranged "from 0 to 93% (mean = 59.7%, SD +/-24.0%)". Groups significantly differed (two-sample t-test, T = 7.74, p < .001). Sample: 14 blind, 12 sighted subjects. The claim's quote correctly attributes "consistently fell below 16%" to sighted and 0-93%/mean 59.7% to blind. The large blind-vs-sighted gap at extreme speed is exactly what the study reports. Source quality is sufficient: peer-reviewed neuroscience study with multiple follow-up publications from the same group (Frontiers 2013, PLOS One 2015). Note: the claim correctly uses "16 syllables/second" (the study's actual metric), not the "16x playback" framing in the research question header. No contradicting evidence found.

### [18] Blind subjects can learn to comprehend accelerated text-to-speech at ultra-fast rates exceeding 16 syllables per second, far above the normal speech range of ~6 syllables/s (normal speaking rate 4-8 syllables/s).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3745084/ (primary)
Quote: "blind subjects can learn to use accelerated text-to-speech systems for 'reading' texts at ultra-fast speaking rates (>16 syllables/s), exceeding by far the normal range of 6 syllables/s"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is verbatim-supported by the primary source and corroborated by multiple independent peer-reviewed papers. The PMC3745084 article (Hertrich, Dietrich, Ackermann, "How can audiovisual pathways enhance the temporal resolution of time-compressed speech in blind subjects?", Frontiers in Psychology, 2013) states verbatim: "blind subjects can learn to use accelerated text-to-speech systems for 'reading' texts at ultra-fast speaking rates (>16 syllables/s), exceeding by far the normal range of 6 syllables/s." Independent corroboration: BMC Neuroscience 2013 (PMC3847124) states blind subjects "understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables per second" vs. "maximum performance level of normal-sighted listeners (ca. 8 syl/s)," with measured comprehension of 59.7% mean for blind listeners vs 9.0% for sighted controls on 16 syl/s material. A separate Frontiers in Human Neuroscience 2013 paper (PMC3805979) documents training-induced (6 months) acquisition of 18 syl/s comprehension in late-blind participants. The normal-rate figure is consistently cited as 4-8 syl/s (one paper says ~6). Source quality (peer-reviewed neuroscience journals, primary empirical fMRI/behavioral studies) matches the claim's strength. CAVEAT/SCOPE NOTE: The claim correctly states "16 syllables per second," NOT "16x playback speed" — these are different quantities. The research question conflates them ("playback speeds greater than 16x"), but the CLAIM itself is accurate about rate in syllables/s. The claim is also narrowly about trained/proficient BLIND subjects, not the general population — which the claim correctly scopes.

### [19] The bottleneck for understanding time-compressed speech in sighted listeners is limited temporary phonological storage capacity (buffering), not a hard speed constraint, and is linked to frontal brain structures.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3745084/ (primary)
Quote: "the 'bottleneck' in sighted subjects for the comprehension of time-compressed speech arises from limited temporary storage capacities for phonological materials rather than speed constraints"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is accurately supported by the cited source AND its underlying primary literature. The quote is verbatim from Hertrich, Dietrich & Ackermann (2013), "How can audiovisual pathways enhance the temporal resolution of time-compressed speech in blind subjects?" (Frontiers in Psychology). The same paper explicitly links the buffer to frontal structures: "The buffer mechanism for the comprehension of continuous speech has been attributed to left inferior frontal gyrus (IFG), anterior insula, precentral cortex, and upper frontal cortex including the supplementary motor area (SMA and pre-SMA)." The underlying PRIMARY source — Vagharchakian et al. (2012), "A Temporal Bottleneck in the Language Comprehension Network," J Neurosci 32(26):9089 — corroborates the substance: a "large modality-independent left-hemispheric language network, including the inferior frontal gyrus... shows a remarkably time-invariant response," and at fast rates "incoming words must be buffered, and intelligibility vanishes when buffer storage and retrieval operations are saturated." Independent corroboration: inserting silent intervals restores intelligibility to sentences compressed at ~33% (argues against a purely acoustic/speed bottleneck, since silence lets the buffer drain), and pSTS/IFG remain time-invariant down to ~40% compression (post-sensory limit). NUANCE / partial caveat: Vagharchakian frames the bottleneck as BOTH a fixed internal processing speed AND buffer saturation — the "fixed speed" is why buffering becomes necessary, while buffer capacity is what determines comprehension failure. So "storage capacity rather than speed constraints" is a slight simplification of a dual mechanism, but it faithfully captures the failure-mode (it is buffer saturation, not an acoustic speed wall, that kills comprehension). Source quality (peer-reviewed J Neurosci primary fMRI study + Frontiers review) matches the non-extraordinary claim; framework is dated (2012-13) but uncontradicted and still cited in 2022 channel-capacity work. No contradicting source found.


## Refuted claims (for transparency)
- "The upper comprehension limit for normal sighted listeners is about 8 syllables per second, far below the ~22 syl/s achieved by trained blind listeners, establishing a perceptual ceiling for untrained accelerated-speech comprehension." (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/, vote 1-2)
- "The intelligibility threshold for time-compressed speech in normal-hearing listeners is approximately 11 to 16 syllables per second, setting a perceptual upper bound for accelerated speech comprehension." (https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/bf03199702, vote 1-2)
- "Listeners can identify accelerated speech with near 100% accuracy when it is time-compressed from a normal rate of about 3-4 syllables per second up to roughly 8 syllables per second." (https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/bf03199702, vote 0-3)
- "Reduced comprehension of time-compressed speech is primarily caused by decreased intelligibility of individual words, not just by the faster delivery rate per se." (https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/BF03208200, vote 0-3)
- "The maximum comprehension rate for normal/untrained sighted listeners is around 8 syllables per second, establishing the perceptual ceiling for non-experts." (https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/human-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnhum.2013.00701/full, vote 1-2)

## Instructions
1. Identify claims that say the same thing — merge them, combine their sources.
2. Group related claims into coherent findings. Each finding should directly address the research question.
3. Assign confidence per finding: high (multiple primary sources, unanimous votes), medium (secondary sources or split votes), low (single source or blog-quality).
4. Write a 3-5 sentence executive summary answering the research question.
5. Note caveats: what's uncertain, what sources were weak, what time-sensitivity applies.
6. List 2-4 open questions that emerged but weren't answered.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
This is a synthesis task. All the research is already done and verified. I need to merge semantic duplicates, group claims into coherent findings, and produce structured output. No file operations needed.

Let me analyze the 20 confirmed claims:

**Group 1 — TSM fundamentals (what speed-listening is):** [0]
**Group 2 — TSM algorithm quality/artifacts:** [1]
**Group 3 — WSOLA mechanism:** [2], plus [10] (WSOLA used in study, pitch-preserving)
**Group 4 — PSOLA:** [8]
**Group 5 — Compression vs naive speed-up intelligibility:** [5]
**Group 6 — Word intelligibility bottleneck under compression:** [6], [14] (intelligibility vs comprehension distinction)
**Group 7 — Perceptual ceiling normal-hearing:** [7], [13] (275 wpm / phonological loop)
**Group 8 — Blind ultra-fast comprehension ~22 syl/s:** [3], [9], [15], [16], [17], [18] (these heavily overlap)
**Group 9 — Training/adaptation effects:** [4], [11], [12]
**Group 10 — Neural bottleneck (buffering):** [19]

Let me merge and synthesize.

input.summary: The foundational operation behind speed-listening is time-scale modification (TSM): changing playback speed without altering pitch. The two classical pitch-preserving algorithms are WSOLA (Waveform Similarity Overlap-Add) and the phase vocoder (PV-TSM); WSOLA improves comprehensibility over plain overlap-add by shifting analysis frames (up to plus or minus delta_max samples) to maximize cross-correlation and align periodic structures, while PSOLA (Pitch Synchronous Overlap-Add) is preferred when one wants to push the speech modulation spectrum to higher modulation frequencies while preserving spectral features. Sampling/TSM-based compression preserves intelligibility at substantially faster rates than naive (pitch-shifting) speed-up, but comprehension still degrades because individually-intelligible words become less intelligible inside the limited time constraints of a compressed message, and because a temporary phonological-storage buffer (not a hard acoustic speed wall) saturates. Regarding extreme speed-listening: the research question's premise of "16x playback" is not what the literature actually measures — researchers measure speech RATE in syllables/second, not playback multiplier. Normal-hearing/sighted listeners hit a perceptual ceiling around 11-16 syllables/second (comprehension begins declining near ~275 words/minute), whereas trained late-blind individuals can reach up to ~22 syllables/second (roughly 3x the ~8 syl/s sighted ceiling), an adaptation acquired through months of daily exposure that produces both a rapid initial phase and a slower, deeper, training-dependent phase of perceptual learning.

findings.claim: Speed-listening rests on time-scale modification (TSM): speeding up (or slowing down) audio playback WITHOUT changing pitch. This is the foundational, pitch-preserving operation that distinguishes intelligible speed-up from naive resampling (which raises pitch).
findings.evidence: Driedger & Muller (peer-reviewed review, Applied Sciences 2016, Int. Audio Laboratories Erlangen): 'Time-scale modification (TSM) is the task of speeding up or slowing down an audio signal's playback speed without changing its pitch.' Corroborated by Stanford CCRMA (J.O. Smith). Multiple sources confirm podcast/audiobook 1.5x-2x speed-up IS TSM, implemented via WSOLA/PSOLA. [merged from claim 0]

findings.claim: WSOLA and the phase vocoder (PV-TSM) preserve perceptual quality of harmonic signals well but introduce audible artifacts on percussive/transient material; no single TSM method handles all signal types, so combining approaches (e.g. harmonic-percussive separation) reduces artifacts. For speech (predominantly harmonic), WSOLA gives very high-quality results.
findings.evidence: Driedger & Muller (Appl. Sci. 2016) verbatim: WSOLA/PV-TSM 'preserv[e] the perceptual quality of harmonic signals to a high degree, but introduce noticeable artifacts when modifying percussive signals... it is possible to substantially reduce artifacts by combining different TSM approaches'; abstract: 'there is no single TSM method that can cope with all kinds of audio signals equally well.' Banai & Lavner (PMC3467231) independently used WSOLA because it 'achieve[s] very high quality time-scale modification of speech signals' and 'maintains acoustic properties like pitch and timbre unchanged.' [merged from claims 1, 10]

findings.claim: WSOLA's comprehensibility advantage over plain OLA comes from allowing small shifts (up to plus/minus delta_max samples) of analysis-frame positions, choosing the shift that maximizes cross-correlation so periodic (harmonic) structures align across overlapping regions, reducing phase-jump artifacts.
findings.evidence: Driedger & Muller Section 4 (verbatim): 'This method's core idea is to allow for slight shifts (of up to +/-delta_max in Z samples) of the analysis frames' positions... compute the optimal shift index that maximizes the cross-correlation... such that periodic structures... are optimally aligned... in the overlapping region.' Original WSOLA paper (Verhelst & Roelands, IEEE 1993) corroborates the waveform-similarity / cross-correlation mechanism. [merged from claim 2]

findings.claim: PSOLA (Pitch Synchronous Overlap-Add) is the alternative TSM choice for uniform time-compression because, as the compression ratio increases, it shifts the speech modulation spectrum toward higher modulation frequencies while preserving the modulation-spectrum shape and spectral features (pitch contour).
findings.evidence: Gransier, van Wieringen & Wouters (J. Assoc. Res. Otolaryngol. 2022, PMC9085996): sentences 'uniformly time-compressed... with the Pitch Synchronous Overlap and Add algorithm (PSOLA)' chosen because it 'shifts the modulation spectrum with increasing compression ratio to higher modulation frequencies... but at the same time preserves the shape of modulation spectrum and the spectral features.' [from claim 8]

findings.claim: Sampling/TSM-based time-compression preserves intelligibility at significantly faster rates than naive speeded (pitch-raising) speed-up: pitch-preserving compression yields a higher speech-rate intelligibility threshold. This is the core empirical justification for using TSM rather than plain fast playback.
findings.evidence: de Haan (1977), Perception & Psychophysics 22:366-372: 'Thresholds for compressed speech were significantly higher than those for speeded speech, an indication that the threshold at the very least reflects intelligibility.' A higher threshold = intelligible at faster rate. Sampling method (periodic segment removal, pitch-preserving) outperforms simple speeded (pitch-raising) playback. Foundational (1977) but consistent with current TSM-over-resampling consensus. [from claim 5]

findings.claim: Comprehension degrades under compression in part because words that are individually intelligible become LESS intelligible when packed inside the limited time constraints of a fast message — a processing-time bottleneck. The literature distinguishes word INTELLIGIBILITY from discourse COMPREHENSION, which compression affects differently.
findings.evidence: Heiman et al. (1986), Perception & Psychophysics 40(6):407-411: 'individually compressed words are less intelligible when presented within the limited time constraints of a compressed message.' Foulke & Sticht (1969 review, Psychol. Bull. 72:50-62) established the intelligibility-vs-comprehension distinction, reviewing methods to generate time-compressed speech and assess effects on word intelligibility vs connected-discourse comprehension. [merged from claims 6, 14]

findings.claim: The bottleneck for time-compressed speech in normal/sighted listeners is limited temporary phonological storage capacity (buffer saturation), NOT a hard acoustic speed constraint, and is linked to left frontal structures (inferior frontal gyrus, anterior insula, precentral cortex, SMA/pre-SMA). Inserting silent intervals to let the buffer drain restores intelligibility.
findings.evidence: Hertrich, Dietrich & Ackermann (Frontiers in Psychology 2013, PMC3745084): the 'bottleneck in sighted subjects... arises from limited temporary storage capacities for phonological materials rather than speed constraints,' attributed to left IFG, anterior insula, precentral cortex, SMA/pre-SMA. Underlying primary source Vagharchakian et al. (2012, J. Neurosci. 32:9089) shows a time-invariant left language network and that intelligibility vanishes when buffer storage/retrieval saturates; silent gaps restore it. Caveat: Vagharchakian frames it as a dual mechanism (fixed internal processing speed forces buffering; buffer capacity determines failure). [from claim 19]

findings.claim: There is a perceptual ceiling for accelerated-speech comprehension in normal-hearing/sighted (untrained) listeners at roughly 11-16 syllables/second; relatedly, comprehension of accelerated speech begins to decline more steeply around ~275 words/minute, a threshold tied (by modern theory) to phonological-loop processing capacity.
findings.evidence: Gransier et al. 2022 (PMC9085996): 'The ability to perceive time-compressed speech in normal-hearing listeners has been reported to be approximately 11 to 16 syllables per second.' Nature Sci. Reports (s41598-023-29755-x): upper limit '11 to 16 syllables per second.' Foulke & Sticht (1969) via 2024 Educ. Psychol. Rev. review: comprehension declines at ~275 wpm, 'speeds that surpass what the phonological loop... can handle.' Caveat: phonological-loop framing is a modern (post-1974 Baddeley) overlay on the 1969 finding. [merged from claims 7, 13]

findings.claim: Trained late-blind/vision-impaired individuals can comprehend ultra-fast (TTS/screen-reader and time-compressed) speech at rates up to ~22 syllables/second — roughly 3x the ~8 syl/s ceiling of sighted listeners and far above the ~4-8 (often cited ~6) syl/s normal speaking rate. This demonstrates the perceptual limit is plastic, not fixed, and shifts with exposure/training.
findings.evidence: Multiple peer-reviewed neuroscience papers from the Dietrich/Hertrich/Ackermann group converge: blind people 'understand spoken language at speaking rates of up to 22 syllables per second' vs untrained ~8 syl/s (PMC3847124, PMC3805979, Frontiers 2013). Original empirical figure: Moos & Trouvain (2007). At 16 syl/s, sighted accuracy 'consistently fell below 16%' (mean 9.0%) while blind averaged 59.7% (range 0-93%) (PMC3847124). Gransier 2022 notes 'special populations perceive speech at rates up to 22 syllables per second... neural processing... can be altered by exposure and training.' 22/8 = ~2.75x ('roughly 3x'). Caveat: 22 syl/s is the trained upper bound; most experiments tested at 16 syl/s. NOTE: the research question's '16x playback' is a category error — literature measures syllables/second, not playback multiplier. [merged from claims 3, 9, 15, 16, 17, 18]

findings.claim: Adaptation to accelerated speech is real and trainable, with a two-phase structure: a rapid initial adaptation (10-20 sentences improves identification but leaves performance well below ceiling, ~40-85% correct) followed by a slower, deeper, training-dependent phase. Months of daily exposure (e.g. via a TTS screen reader) produce large, lasting gains — four of six trained blind participants reached ~70% word comprehension at 18 syl/s.
findings.evidence: Banai & Lavner (2012, PLoS ONE, PMC3467231, 'Perceptual Learning of Time-Compressed Speech: More than Rapid Adaptation'): 'Brief adaptation (10-20 sentences) significantly improved identification, but left performance well below perfect (40-85% correct)'; 'Multiday training... results in significantly more perceptual learning than brief adaptation,' progressing from a rapid phase to a 'prolonged and more stimulus specific phase' (trained d=1.37 vs control d=0.56). Dietrich et al. (2013, Frontiers Hum. Neurosci., PMC3805979): ~6 months >=1hr/day JAWS+Eloquence TTS training; 'Four of six participants showed... considerable enhancement of ultra-fast speech perception (about 70% points correctly repeated words)' at 18 syl/s (from <14% pre-training). [merged from claims 4, 11, 12]

input.caveats: PREMISE MISMATCH (most important): The research question asks about playback speeds 'greater than 16x' and 'extreme speed-ups.' The peer-reviewed literature does NOT operate in playback-multiplier units — it measures speech RATE in syllables/second (or words/minute). 16 syllables/second is roughly 2-3x a normal speaking rate (~6-8 syl/s), NOT 16x. There is no credible scientific evidence for intelligible speech comprehension at anything like 16x normal playback; the documented human ceiling is ~22 syl/s (about 3x normal) and only for highly trained blind listeners. Claims of '16x speed listening' in the consumer/forum sphere conflate playback multiplier with rate and were not substantiated by any verified primary source. TIME-SENSITIVITY: TSM signal-processing fundamentals (WSOLA 1993, PSOLA, phase vocoder) and the human-perception/psychophysics findings (1969-2022) are in mature, stable fields; the 1969/1977/1986 foundational papers remain uncontested, so age is not disqualifying. Modern neural/DSP work (2012-2023) updates but does not overturn them. SOURCE STRENGTH: All findings rest on peer-reviewed primary sources (MDPI Applied Sciences review, Springer Perception & Psychophysics, Frontiers, BMC/J. Neurosci., PLoS ONE, J. Assoc. Res. Otolaryngol., Nature Sci. Reports); no marketing/forum/blog sources were used in confirmed claims. POPULATION SCOPE: The most extreme rates (~22 syl/s) are specific to trained late-blind individuals and recruit visual cortex/fusiform/pulvinar via cross-modal plasticity — they do not generalize to the sighted general population. Five claims were refuted in adversarial review (mostly over-precise restatements of the 8 syl/s 'ceiling' and threshold figures), so single-figure 'ceilings' should be read as approximate ranges, not hard constants.
openQuestions: What modern intelligibility-enhancement and prosody/spectral-processing techniques (beyond classical WSOLA/PSOLA TSM) — e.g. dynamic silence/pause compression, adaptive per-phoneme compression, formant/clarity enhancement, or neural-network-based TSM — most improve comprehension at high speed-up factors? The verified corpus covered TSM algorithms and human perceptual limits but did not surface evidence on these specific enhancement methods named in the question.

Does inserting periodic silent gaps to let the phonological buffer drain (shown to restore intelligibility for sighted listeners at high compression) translate into a deployable consumer 'speed-listening' technique, and what gap timing/ratio is optimal?

Can the months-long training-driven adaptation observed in blind listeners be replicated in sighted users with deliberate practice, and how far above the ~11-16 syl/s sighted ceiling can a motivated trained sighted listener realistically reach?

How do non-uniform (content-aware / variable-rate) compression algorithms compare to uniform PSOLA/WSOLA for comprehension at extreme rates, given PSOLA was specifically chosen in one study for its UNIFORM modulation-spectrum behavior?

content.text:
I have completed the synthesis. The 20 confirmed claims were merged into 10 coherent findings addressing the research question, organized into three thematic areas: (1) TSM signal-processing fundamentals and algorithms (WSOLA, PV-TSM, PSOLA), (2) the empirical case for pitch-preserving compression over naive speed-up and the word-intelligibility/buffer bottlenecks, and (3) human perceptual limits and training/adaptation effects for accelerated speech.

The most important caveat I flagged: the research question's premise of "16x playback" is a category error — the scientific literature measures speech rate in syllables/second, not playback multiplier, and the documented human ceiling (~22 syl/s, trained blind listeners only) is roughly 3x normal, with no credible evidence for anything resembling 16x.
